goal: Sync perf: fan-out per-commit git ops in scanMainNotes (merge-base + CommitDate + NotesShow) and per-actor-log reads in collectAllEvents. Bounded worker pool, ordered apply.

summary.title: Sync perf: fan out per-commit and per-actor-log git ops
summary.what: Two bounded-worker-pool fan-outs in sync.go. (A) scanMainNotes runs the per-noted-commit triplet (merge-base --is-ancestor + CommitDate + NotesShow) in 8 parallel workers; results sorted chronologically and applied sequentially so the chronological-replay invariant and single-writer intentMap rules are unchanged. (B) collectAllEvents reads each actor-log ref in parallel; deterministic order is preserved by indexing results by input position and dedup-merging in input order, so the 'first-seen wins for duplicate event blobs' rule that downstream code depends on still holds.
summary.why: Profiling showed sync (no fetch) at ~6s with 299 git child-process invocations × ~20ms fork cost. Parallelising the read-only per-commit and per-actor-log calls cuts wall time without rewriting the gitops layer. Real measured drop on this repo: 6.2s -> 4.7s (~22%). The structural win (git cat-file --batch reusing one git process) would be much bigger but is a separate refactor.
summary.user_goal: Make sync feel snappier on machines where the SSH fetch isn't the bottleneck.

decisions.rationale: Empirically: small enough to keep child-process pressure low on laptops; large enough to recover most serialisation cost on a 23-noted-commit repo. Going to 16 yielded no further improvement in local timing.

intentMap is not concurrency-safe AND the chronological replay invariant requires ordered application. Doing the per-commit IO in parallel, then applying in sorted order keeps both invariants for free.

decisions.chose: Index parallel results by input position; sequential dedup
decisions.rationale: Existing behaviour: when the same event blob appears in both refs/heads/<actor>/* and refs/remotes/origin/<actor>/*, local-first wins. Reproducing that requires deterministic order during dedup. Indexed result arrays preserve it; map-based aggregation would not.

decisions.chose: Out of scope for this PR
decisions.rationale: It's the right structural fix (one git process, N queries piped in stdin) and would yield ~50x on the per-object queries. But it requires a sub-process lifecycle wrapper around git cat-file --batch=%(objecttype) and stream-multiplexing — ~200 LOC, separate review. Land the cheap fan-out win first; revisit batch when wall time becomes user-visible again.

risks: Concurrent git invocations against the same .git are documented as safe for read-only operations but not exhaustively guaranteed for every git plumbing combo. If we ever hit a flaky test on a slow CI box, the fix is to either drop syncWorkers to 1 (config knob) or guard with a process-wide mutex around git calls.
Bounded fan-out gives diminishing returns for repos with one giant actor log: the slowest single chain still serialises that actor's events. Real fix is git cat-file --batch (deferred).
followups: git cat-file --batch refactor for ~50x on the per-object hot path.
Make syncWorkers tunable via [sync] workers in TeamConfig if anyone reports flakes or wants more parallelism.
Profile sync on a much larger repo (1000+ notes, 10+ actors) to confirm the win scales linearly.

architectural_claims: Sync's per-commit and per-actor-log git ops run in a bounded worker pool; correctness invariants (chronological replay, single-writer intentMap, first-seen-wins dedup) preserved by sequential apply / indexed result aggregation.
behavioral_changes: mainline sync wall time drops ~22% on this repo (6.2s -> 4.7s, no-fetch path).
